etymology.description: 武(武) (wǔ, "martial; military") + 昌(昌) (chāng, "prosperous") + 起(起) (qǐ, "rise; start") + 義(義) (yì, "righteous; cause")
etymology.interpretation: 武(武) (wǔ, "martial; military") indicates the involvement of military action. 昌(昌) (chāng, "prosperous") can hint at a flourishing or significant place, specifically referring to Wuchang, a key city. 起(起) (qǐ, "rise; start") signifies the action of beginning or rising. 義(義) (yì, "righteous; cause") conveys the idea of a just cause or movement, emphasizing the moral or political justification behind the uprising. Combined, this refers to the historical revolt known as the Wuchang Uprising, which was a pivotal event leading to the Xinhai Revolution and the fall of the Qing Dynasty.
